coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and upscaling of silicon nanoparticles for lithium-ion batteries in a hot-wall reactor

coredata.dc:creator: Loewenich, Moritz
Orthner, Hans
Wollny, Patrick
Wlokas, Irenaeus
Bade, Stefan
Lyubina, Julia
Wiggers, Hartmut
coredata.dc:description: Small amounts of silicon are currently used to partly replace graphite as anode material in lithium-ion batteries to increase their energy density and fast charging capability. Ensuring mechanical integrity during charging and discharging as well as the production of suitable silicon-based materials on a large scale at acceptable costs still remains a challenge. In this work, the formation of silicon nanoparticles from the gas phase is analyzed to understand the process, identify key parameters regarding a suitable process technology, and resolve upscaling issues for transfer to the industrial pilot scale. The experimental findings are supported by simulations of fluid dynamics. Process conditions regarding the formation of pure as well carbon containing nanoparticles are studied and a successful transfer to an industrial pilot plant reactor is demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructured materials
Gas-solid reactions
Computer simulations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Particle synthesis in a research pilot plant (RPP)
Particle synthesis in an industrial pilot plant (IPP)
Comparison of a-SiCx synthesis in the research pilot plant and the industrial pilot plant

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: German Ministry of Economy and Climate Action

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: In memory of Vitalij K. Pecharsky, an outstanding scientist, wise and witty colleague. This work was supported by the German Ministry of Economy and Climate Action [grant numbers 03EI3027A and 03EI3027B] The Authors thank the Interdisciplinary Center for Analytics on the Nanoscale (ICAN), UDE for TEM Microscopy. XRD measurements were kindly performed by Ali Sheikh.

Julia Lyubina reports financial support was provided by Evonik Operations GmbH. Stefan Bade reports financial support was provided by Evonik Operations GmbH. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Funding: This work was supported by the German Ministry of Economy and Climate Action [grant numbers 03EI3027A and 03EI3027B] The Authors thank the Interdisciplinary Center for Analytics on the Nanoscale (ICAN), UDE for TEM Microscopy. XRD measurements were kindly performed by Ali Sheikh.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General process scheme, starting with the precursor gases, showing the decomposition of monosilane in the hot-wall reactor with silicon nanoparticles as the product. Ethylene can be added to form a-SiCx nanoparticles.
TEM images from selected samples produced at 615 °C with different concentrations of monosilane (left) and example of how the size distribution of the primary particles is determined (right).

Boxplots of the primary-particle size distributions (PSDs) extracted from TEM analysis of samples with various precursor concentrations for two different temperatures (experiments from RPP: A1/2-D1/2). All PSDs have a monomodal lognormal shape. Samples produced at 615 °C are plotted in green, those produced at 665 °C in orange.

SSA over SiH4 concentration in the oven. Black and red line are for the RPP-samples A-D (615 °C and 665 °C), while the blue line represents the IPP-sample and the green dot a comparison experiment from the RPP.

a) FTIR spectra of powder samples RPP-A1–D1 produced at different monosilane concentrations at 615 °C; b) Hydrogen content of the 4 selected samples, measured by elemental analysis and plotted over the equivalent BET size.

SiH4 consumption and amorphous fraction for samples from the RPP (A-D) depicted over precursor concentration for 2 different temperatures, a) 615 °C and b) 665 °C; The right part of both graphs shows 2 experiments each with 10% precursor and reduced residence time.

a,b) instantaneous flow fields of flow velocities in the HWR extracted from computational fluid dynamics simulations, indicating backflow of gases in the area of the nozzle (bright blue to white code, negative velocity) a) without sheath-plate and b) with sheath-plate c) Sketch of the nozzle area, configurations with sheath gas plate (right) and without (left) are shown; d) Set of temperature profiles used for studies on the synthesis of particles with different temperature-time profiles.
Boxplots of primary particle diameters obtained from TEM analysis for different heating-profiles and reactor-geometries in the RPP.
Carbon content in produced powders measured by elemental analysis plotted over carbon concentration in the precursor gas mixture. Process conditions are given in the caption.
a, b) TEM-Pictures of Sample RPP-1 at two magnifications, c,d) pictures from the sample IPP-1, produced at the same synthesis conditions with the same magnifications.
a) SiH4 consumption, b) BET specific surface area, c) carbon content, d) amorphous fraction of samples produced at the same conditions in the RPP and IPP.

Sample names and synthesis conditions for the RPP experiments. Additionally, residence time, precursor consumption (ξ), BET specific surface area (SSA) and amorphousness of the produced materials are shown.

Sample names and synthesis conditions for the RPP and IPP experiments towards a-SiCx composite materials. Additionally, residence time, precursor consumption (ξ), BET specific surface area (SSA) and amorphousness of the produced materials are shown.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and upscaling of silicon nanoparticles for lithium-ion batteries in a hot-wall reactor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Small amounts of silicon are currently used to partly replace graphite as anode material in lithium-ion batteries to increase their energy density and fast charging capability. Ensuring mechanical integrity during charging and discharging as well as the production of suitable silicon-based materials on a large scale at acceptable costs still remains a challenge. In this work, the formation of silicon nanoparticles from the gas phase is analyzed to understand the process, identify key parameters regarding a suitable process technology, and resolve upscaling issues for transfer to the industrial pilot scale. The experimental findings are supported by simulations of fluid dynamics. Process conditions regarding the formation of pure as well carbon containing nanoparticles are studied and a successful transfer to an industrial pilot plant reactor is demonstrated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data from UDE are available, data from EVONIK may be confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With a growing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIB) for mobile devices and electromobility, high energy-density battery materials are of great interest to reduce overall weight and size and to increase running time and range. Partial or even complete replacement of graphite, the active component on the anode side of lithium-ion batteries, by silicon is the near-term option to meet the industry demands. Substituting graphite (specific capacity of 372 mAh/g), which is still the most common anode material in use today [1], [2] by silicon (3579 mAh/g) can significantly increase the anode capacity. To ensure the required electrochemical and mechanical stability, silicon particle size should be preferably reduced to the sub-micron range. On the other side – due to unwanted but unavoidable parasitic surface reactions – the silicon particles should have a low specific surface area (SSA) to enable high first cycle Coulombic efficiency (FCE). In coarse silicon particles, the mechanical stress during charging and discharging cannot be compensated and the particles tend to crack due to the enormous volume expansion of up to 300% on lithiation [3–5]. Cracking causes poor cycling performance as repeatedly new surfaces are generated leading to additional parasitic reactions, and the overall performance and electrode integrity are lost. This can be mitigated somewhat by the use of amorphous nanoparticles as opposed to the crystalline ones [6–9]. Also, the size control of silicon nanomaterials for battery applications is crucial [10], [11]. In addition, surface passivation/functionalization is a further way to reduce the unwanted surface reactions [12–14].

There are different manufacturing processes for the provision of suitable silicon grades. Some groups investigate saw dust from semiconductor industry or start with metallurgical silicon, which is milled to a micro- or nano size [15], [16]. Though very cheap, particle morphology and purity are hard to control. Other groups are investigating atomization and condensation of silicon in plasma reactors, which, however, requires a high amount of electrical energy. Furthermore, wet synthesis routes [17] using reduction-reactions to synthesize silicon from SiO2 are reported [18]. All approaches can yield preferred, sophisticated structures, but especially the latter is challenging for large scale production due to multi-step batch processing involved. As an alternative, bottom-up approach, gas-phase synthesis of silicon is possible by laser pyrolysis [19–22], plasma synthesis [23–25] or thermal decomposition in free-space reactors [26–30], typically using monosilane (SiH4) as precursor. There are also some reports on the pyrolysis of the less cost intensive SiCl4 in a plasma processes [31], [32], however, the material applicability for LIB and purity are questionable so far.

Apart from the morphology, the structure is another key parameter of the silicon nanomaterials. While both crystalline and amorphous particles are being developed, there are some beneficial properties reported for amorphous particles, e.g. higher resistance to fracture [33–35], faster lithiation kinetics [36], [37] and prevention of detrimental crystalline c-Li15Si4 phase formation [38]. A possibility for inhibiting the crystallization of silicon during particle formation and growth and ensuring surface passivation, the addition of carbon or nitrogen precursors during the gas phase pyrolysis of monosilane has shown some advantages. Gradient structures with a sub-stoichiometric overall composition of either a-SiCx or SiNx are formed by their heterogeneous decomposition on the surface of freshly formed silicon particles [9]. As a result, amorphous particles with good performance in the LIB can be produced even at temperatures at which pure silicon would have already crystallized, so that high production rates are possible with a quantitative conversion of the expensive monosilane [30–39], [40].

The aim of this work is to investigate the formation of amorphous silicon nanoparticles in a hot-wall reactor (HWR) and to study the parameters required for the most complete possible conversion of the precursor SiH4. In addition, the formation of amorphous a-SiCx by addition of ethylene is also investigated. With the support of fluid dynamic calculations, key parameters are identified which were applied for transfer to industrial pilot scale. Silicon particles are produced at various conditions with the design goals being a narrow particle size distribution (PSD) around 150 nm, fully amorphous material and a high SiH4 decomposition rate. By varying precursor concentration, residence time and temperature, the complex relation between SiH4 decomposition, homogenous- and heterogenous nucleation, coagulation, and the flow-field is studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle synthesis in a research pilot plant (RPP)
ce:section.ce:para: The general setup of the research pilot plant HWR for thermal decomposition of monosilane is described elsewhere [41] and a scheme of the overall setup is provided in the supplementary information. Briefly, a vertically arranged quartz tube with an inner diameter of 112 mm is placed inside a 1 m long resistively heated oven with five 100 mm and one 500 mm separately controllable heating zones ( Fig. 1). Monosilane (SiH4, UHP, Air Liquide) is introduced into the quartz tube via a water-cooled nozzle while argon is fed into the reactor as a coaxial sheath gas around the nozzle. All gases are supplied by mass flow controllers and the silicon powder production rate can reach up to 1 kg/h. For some experiments with the aim of producing a-SiCx, ethylene (C2H4, 2.6, Air Liquide) is added together with monosilane. Ethylene and monosilane concentrations in the reaction zone are varied between 0 – 10% and 0.3 – 25% respectively. The reactor pressure can be adjusted by a pumping unit. The wall temperatures inside the reactor were measured in separate experiments by gluing type-K thermocouples to the quartz-tube. The powder is separated from the off-gas by a sinter-metal filter and can be harvested under inert conditions in a glovebox.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle synthesis in an industrial pilot plant (IPP)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of silicon nanoparticles is performed by thermal decomposition of the precursor gases monosilane and a mixture of monosilane and ethylene in case of a-SiCx formation, respectively, in a custom-made hot-wall reactor industrial pilot plant (IPP). The apparatus consists of a gas delivering system, a tubular hot-wall flow reactor consisting of five parallel tubes and a powder separator for collecting the particles. All gases are fed into the reactor by using mass flow controllers. All experiments are performed at 1.2 bar abs. The heating length is 3000 mm and the inner diameter of the reactor tube is 192 mm, which corresponds to 3 times the cross-sectional area compared to the RPP. The powder synthesis rate is in the range of several kg/h for every reactor tube. The temperature of the reactor is adjusted between 630 and 850 °C and the residence time t in the reactor is varied between 3 and 20 s. The silane and ethylene concentration in the gas mixture were varied between 10 – 40 vol% (monosilane) and 0 – 13.6 vol% (ethylene) of the total gas in the reaction zone. At the outlet of the reactor the silane concentration was analyzed continuously using an SiH4 infrared sensor

Structure and powder morphology studies were performed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The used instruments include a FEI Tecnai F20 operated at 200 kV and a Cs corrected JEOL JEM-2200FS TEM/STEM using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol and deposited on holey carbon TEM grids. Specific surface area was determined by nitrogen absorption using BET method (Micromeritics, TriStar II Plus). Prior to the measurement, samples were dried under vacuum at 150 °C for 20 min.

The phase composition and structure parameters were determined by Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data collected in the 2θ range of 15 – 110° with a step size of 0.05° using a Philips X’Pert Pro diffractometer (Cu-Kα radiation) operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. For amorphous phase fraction determination, XRD patterns were collected on pure amorphous and pure crystalline samples. Furthermore, a powder mixture of the pure amorphous sample and pure crystalline sample was prepared and analyzed. For the quantification of the crystalline fractions, the "Full Pattern Autoscale" method implemented in Philips' HighScore Plus evaluation software was used. In this method, the diffraction pattern of the sample is adjusted using two (or more) reference patterns by scaling the intensities of the reference until the best match with the measurement diffractogram is achieved using a least squares method. The Full Pattern Autoscale method was shown to deliver results with good precision and relatively good accuracy. Typically, a standard deviation of 0.7 wt% and a relative standard deviation of about 3% are observed. The total carbon content, defined as the concentration averaged over the whole powder volume, is determined using elemental analysis by combustion (Leco, CS744). Monosilane concentration in the off-gas was monitored by IR-Absorption (ABB, Limas 11).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis is conducted with a Cs corrected JEOL JEM-2200FS using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol and deposited on carbon TEM grids. To determine the primary particle size distribution (PSD), roughly around 300 primary particles per sample are measured by hand ( Fig. 2). The specific surface area of the samples is analyzed using the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller method (BET) with a Quantachrome NOVA2200 after the powders were dried at 120 °C in vacuum for 16 hours.

The microstructure of the particles was analyzed via X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANanlytical, X’Pert) using Cu Kα radiation. Crystallinity is determined by the comparison of the area under the crystalline peak and the amorphous halo, the results are comparable to those obtained using the method described above. The materials are also analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, Vertex 80) in an ATR setup. The off gas of the reactor is analyzed continuously by a quadrupole mass spectrometer (QMS, IPI, GAM200). The total hydrogen and carbon amount of as-synthesized powders is determined via elemental analysis (ELTRA, ONH-p and ELTRA, CS-I, respectively) by combustion and analyzing the off-gas.

In a first set of experiments, selected studies on the formation of Si nanoparticles under variation of temperature, residence time, and precursor concentration are performed in the RPP, and the properties of the produced materials are analyzed. In addition, fluid dynamics simulations are carried out for selected process conditions in order to investigate the effect of flow effects and, in particular, reverse flows.

Using selected examples, the change in particle morphology as a function of precursor concentration will first be shown using two selected temperatures. A full list of all experiments discussed in this first section is given in Table 1. The left TEM pictures in Fig. 2a-d) clearly show (using the same magnification) the concentration-dependent growth of aggregates and primary particles with increasing precursor concentration. The primary particle sizes of the different samples are determined as shown in Fig. 2e), the yellow bars indicate the respective particle diameters. Although manual determination of primary particle sizes using TEM images is prone to error due to the limited number of particles analyzed and differences between operators, this method was chosen as it is the only way to determine primary particle sizes in the sometimes heavily sintered aggregates. Studies with different operators found deviations of around +- 14% [42]. Alternatively determining the size automatically, which has been shown in literature [43], is deemed difficult, due to the heavily aggregated and partly non-spherical structures together with the contrast from the TEM images. The trends are also found in the BET measurements, although the mean particle sizes determined from these are significantly higher due to the heavy sintering.

Coagulation is observed for high precursor concentrations (Fig. 2). Yet the particle morphology indicates that sintering occurs, and most particles show an aggregate structure with sinter-neck like features. Since extensive sintering is not to be expected at 615 °C, we conclude that mainly coagulation, followed by heterogenous nucleation of monosilane on the surface of freshly nucleated particles is responsible for the structures found. Due to the slow decomposition kinetics of the monosilane at this temperature, heterogeneous growth is clearly favored compared to homogeneous nucleation, which is further enhanced by the decrease in monosilane concentration as the reaction proceeds. This leads to significantly larger primary particles at higher concentrations. The interpretation is undermined by detailed simulations which combine fluid dynamics, precursor decomposition and particle growth [44].

As observed by the TEM, the primary particle size increases with higher precursor concentration (Figs. 1 and 2). This is explainable by an increased heterogenous nucleation and aggregation due to higher particle densities in the decomposition zone. However, when comparing the results for 615 and 665°C reactor temperature, it is apparent that at the lowest concentration the mean primary particle size is surprisingly small (23 nm) while at the same time a very broad particle size distribution is observed (orange boxplots of experiment with 0.3 and 1% silane). The small primary particles can be explained by faster decomposition kinetics associated with high supersaturation and rapid nucleation of the smallest particles. With respect to the broad distributions, undesirable side effects can occur, presumably backflow, which is associated with significantly higher residence times of some particles and stronger sintering. SI 2 shows TEM results for experiments with reduced residence times, induced by a higher sheath gas stream or reduced pressure. Both show a reduced particle size and reduced width of the PSD, most probably due to suppression of backflows at higher velocities. Furthermore, the results regarding the mean particle sizes are supported by measurements of the specific surface area (left graph in
Fig. 4). Here, too, a clear correlation between concentration and specific surface area can be seen, also confirming the trend with respect to temperature (red graph, 665 °C vs. black graph, 615 °C). While the right part in general already shows good agreement in the trends from RPP and IPP, this shall be discussed in more detail later.

The surface of freshly produced silicon from monosilane is typically hydrogen terminated and undergoes natural oxidation when exposed to air. Both, hydrogen termination as well as surface oxidation can be detected by FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 5a shows the FTIR spectra of samples RPP-A1, B1, C1 and D1 (0.3–10% SiH4). For Sample RPP-A1, with the smallest particles, the highest Si–O signal intensity (800 cm−1) is observed indicating a substantial surface oxidation but also the highest signal intensity for Si–H. For the samples made at higher concentration the signal intensities gradually get smaller. These results again confirm the large surface-to-volume ratio of materials produced at low precursor concentration, which is also supported by elemental analysis for hydrogen, see Fig. 5b. In this figure, the measured hydrogen content is plotted against a theoretical mean particle size calculated from the BET measurements assuming monodisperse, spherical particles.

An essential criterion regarding the applicability of the produced silicon nanoparticles in lithium-ion batteries and the profitability of the production process is the content of amorphous product and the turnover of the precursor used. To determine the structure of the synthesized samples, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Figure SI 9) of materials produced between 615 and 715 °C are analyzed accordingly. Only for some samples produced at 665, 690 and 715 °C the onset of crystallization can be observed, all other samples are completely amorphous. Fig. 6 shows the determined amorphous fraction and the monosilane consumption for the materials produced at 615 and 665 °C. Both graphs show the same trend: monosilane conversion decreases with increasing concentration. In addition, at 665 °C, the crystalline fraction increases at low concentrations. Previous experiments at temperatures > 665 °C and the same average residence time (approx. 5 s) have shown that significant amounts of crystalline silicon are formed in all cases. It was therefore investigated in the present study to what extent a reduced residence time is crucial in avoiding crystallization (experiments on the right of both graphs, residence time between 1.6 and 1.8 s). The results in Table 1 show that this is nearly successful, but at the expense of monosilane conversion. To summarize, we find a rather narrow temperature window of ∼ 50°C between a satisfactory high precursor consumption (min. 615 °C) and the onset of crystallization for pure silicon nanoparticles (starting at 665 °C). For shorter residence times of about 1.7 s, no temperature can be found which matches both conditions.

As previously discussed, one conjecture for a very broad particle size distribution is the occurrence of undesirable backflow, resulting in partially high residence times and thus prolonged particle growth, sintering, and aggregation. A very simplistic method for determining the average residence time in tubular reactors is to assume a plug flow, which is also the basis for the data in Table 1. Yet, plug flow conditions are not applicable for larger diameters and low velocities, especially not when large temperature gradients occur. A coupled particle dynamics simulation shows a major impact of the individual particle time histories on their formation and growth. Those phenomena cannot be captured by simplified plug flow assumptions nor by time averaged flow fields [44]. The same methodology from Wollny et al. was used in the present simulation even though chemical kinetics as well as particle dynamics models were omitted in order to generate averaged flow fields with sufficiently long averaging times (∼100 s). The velocity distributions on the cross-sectional planes shown in Fig. 7a (negative velocities are indicated in light blue to white) reveal strong recirculation zones which penetrate the area behind the central nozzle in the case without sheet-plate. The backflow is induced due to the density gradients caused by heating and precursor decomposition. The resulting buoyancy forces are larger than the inertia of the main flow, causing a transient, chaotic but still not turbulent flow profile in the entire reactor. The buoyancy becomes such strong that a part of the already particle laden flow recirculates into the regime upstream of the nozzle, and after that back into the precursor decomposition zone. This is easily confirmed through particle deposition in this region of the reactor. High collision rates with the freshly formed small nuclei can lead to a further growth and therefore a drastically increased particle size for a small fraction of the particles [44]. Additionally, the hot surface of the particles leads to direct growth if monosilane or its gaseous decomposition products are present. This growth process is supposed to be the main reason for the sinter-neck like structures observed (Fig. 2).

Since the resulting broad PSD is undesired for battery applications, there are different options to diminish the backflow. One can increase the flow velocity, and thereby reduce the ratio of backflow to plug-flow velocities, which, however – with an existing reactor geometry – has a negative effect on precursor conversion due to decreased residence time. Thus, to reduce recirculation, the flow at the end of the nozzle is manipulated by a sheath gas plate which reduces the effective open cross section area for the sheath gas and locally increases the sheath gas velocity. Results of fluid-dynamic simulations without and with the sheath gas plate are displayed in Fig. 7a) and b), a schematic image is shown in c). A snapshot of the simulation without the plate shows a strong updraft to the right of the nozzle, confirming the backflow hypothesis. With the sheath gas plate, backflows are successfully suppressed and thereby largely reduced in spatial extension. More detailed Figures including averaged and instantaneous flow fields are presented in SI 3 and SI 4. In a further experiment, the sheath-gas was preheated to 600 °C by an additional heating device installed (increase at T2, Fig. 7c), and the nozzle temperature (T3) was increased from 80 °C to 350 °C.

In a next set of experiments, the new nozzle geometry was combined with different heating-profiles (rising or falling temperature profiles, Fig. 7d) to further investigate whether they affect particle formation and growth and thus the particle size distribution. The different heating profiles when the gases enter the reactor lead to changes in gas density, which affect the flow profile as well as the particle growth. In literature, zero dimensional studies for the flow in a small-scale tube reactor demonstrated that the heating profile has a significant effect of the particle size and agglomerate morphology [45], [46]. When comparing particle growth for a falling temperature profile (down) with a rising temperature profile (up), the “up” profile results in an aerosol with smaller primary as well as agglomerate particles than the “down” profile. This can give an indication of trends, however, the flow conditions for the larger RPP reactor are far more complex than in an idealized laboratory reactor with a small tube diameter. Nevertheless, both, the effect of the different temperature profiles and that of the sheath gas plate, including preheating of the sheath gas, are investigated. The results of the TEM analyses regarding the particle sizes are shown in Fig. 8. For the setup without sheath gas plate (and thus significant backflow around the nozzle), a trend of larger primary particle sizes for the “down” temperature distributions can be observed and is thus in agreement with the simulations of Winterer [46]. However, the results are clearly different with the sheath gas plate. Here, the “down 2” profile shows the smallest particles while the PSD for the “flat” and the “up” profile are almost identical. Overall, the effects are smaller than expected which on the one hand is due to the locally very different flows in all cases, and on the other hand also to the relatively low temperature gradients in the heating zone of only a little more than 100 °C/m. However, the sheath gas plate in combination with the pre-heating shows an effect, which could also be confirmed by fluid dynamic calculations. While the simulations show that the recirculation is avoided in the nozzle zone, it still occurs in the reaction zone downstream the nozzle. Furthermore, the effectiveness of the sheath gas plate could be confirmed visually after experiments by the extent to which fresh powder was deposited in the uppermost part of the reaction tube. With the plate, powder was only found downstream the plate while it covers the entire reaction tube without a plate installed.

As discussed above, there is only a narrow process window to produce amorphous silicon nanoparticles with the most quantitative conversion of the silicon precursor possible. As known from the literature [30], the temperature range can be extended by adding a carbon precursor, because the incorporation of carbon into the particles hinders the crystallization of silicon. To this goal, ethylene was injected together with monosilane as a precursor. With the carbon doping, amorphous particles can be obtained at temperatures up to 840 °C.

To study the carbon incorporation into silicon, experiments with different ethylene:monosilane ratios between 0 and 16.8 wt% carbon are conducted in the RPP. The as-produced materials are investigated regarding their carbon content by combustion analysis. Fig. 9a) shows the carbon concentration in the produced powders plotted over the carbon concentration in the precursor gas.

The relation between carbon concentration in the precursor mixture and in the final product is approximately linear with a slope of 0.6, indicating that only 60% of the ethylene is consumed, which is also confirmed by QMS measurements of the reactor off-gas during synthesis. As reported previously, ethylene itself is not supposed to decompose at the temperatures selected for these experiments [30]. Thus, the incorporation of carbon into the silicon presumably takes place either via a reaction with intermediate species formed during monosilane decomposition (e.g. SiH2) or via heterogeneous decomposition on the surface of freshly formed nanoparticle surfaces.

Based on the process variations studied here, synthesis conditions were identified that were transferred to the IPP and studied comparatively with the RPP. The results of selected syntheses from both reactors are therefore discussed together in the following.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of a-SiCx synthesis in the research pilot plant and the industrial pilot plant
ce:section.ce:para: A temperature window of 670–800 °C was selected for the operation of the IPP with a view to operating conditions favorable in terms of industrial production technology. From previous studies it is known that crystallization of silicon can be avoided even at 800 °C, when the concentration of ethylene is in the range of 15% of the concentration of monosilane. Thus, experiments were conducted with an ethylene to monosilane ratio of 0.15, leading to carbon contents in the product of 4.5 – 6.5 wt% typically, which indicates a slightly lower ethylene consumption compared to the RPP. The parameter space investigated is presented in Table 2.

A first comparison of the produced materials shows a surprisingly good agreement of the particle morphology of both reactors. Fig. 10a and b show TEM pictures of samples from the RPP synthesized at 670 °C (RPP-1) while Fig. 10 c) and d) show pictures from the IPP produced at identical conditions (IPP-1). In general, both materials consist of agglomerated and aggregated spherical primary particles with similar size and geometry. The other comparative studies also show good agreements in almost all the characteristics analyzed, as shown below by a few examples.

Experiments with a high monosilane concentration, relevant for high production rates are chosen. Temperature and residence time are varied, with one centerpoint. Overall, syntheses at five conditions (the first and last are the same) are conducted and the results of various analyses are summarized in Fig. 11 (the first and last synthesis in the respective figures were performed under identical process conditions).

In addition to the reactor temperature of 735 °C with a residence time of 6 s (samples 15 and 18), materials from experiments at 670 °C and 8 and 4 s residence time (samples 1 and 2) as well as at 800 °C with also 8 and 4 s residence time (samples 10 and 8) are studied. For all experiments, monosilane consumption is in general very high around 99.5%, which is desired, and the trend is comparable for both plants. Yet most values are 0.5–1% lower for the RPP.

The biggest differences between the two reactors are for the SSA. These values are systematically higher for materials from the IPP than for the RPP and are only similar for the experiments at 670 °C. A possible explanation could be a narrower distribution of residence times in the RPP due to the lower tube diameter, or a more efficient (faster) heat transfer to the monosilane for the same reason. The more sluggish transfer of heat to the core of the reaction tube in the IPP could also explain the lower silane consumptions found in the RPP. Further experiments in the IPP show a clear but small increase of the BET area from 8 – 9 m2/g to around 11 – 12 m2/g if the temperature is increased from 670 to 800 °C. Here, temperature is found to be the most deciding parameter for the SSA. RPP experiments confirm this trend.

The carbon content of the produced samples as a function of temperature and residence time follows exactly the same trend. It is very clear for both reactors that higher temperatures as well as higher residence times favor carbon incorporation. Yet now the RPP shows values around 1.2 wt% higher in general, reaching values around 7.5 wt%. Here again temperature effects such as a colder core flow in case of the IPP could play a role, leading to slower decomposition of ethylene. Therefore also applies to the incorporation of carbon that temperature is again the most critical variable, although dependencies here are less strong.

The amount of the crystalline silicon is generally a bit higher for the IPP with values around 1% crystalline fraction, while for the RPP essentially 100% amorphous material is observed. This correlates with the reduced carbon content, with carbon acting as a crystallization inhibitor. One outlier with strongly increased crystallinity for the IPP is overshadowed by the again widely matching trendlines. Overall, however, a surprisingly good agreement of the product qualities from RPP and IPP can be observed.

In summary, a successful transfer of the synthesis of amorphous SiCx alloys applicable for lithium-ion batteries from a research reactor to an industrial pilot plant scale was shown. Specific materials properties such as particle morphology, amount of the crystalline phase, precursor consumption and carbon content were studied depending on process parameters such as temperature, precursor concentration and mean residence time. In a set of comparison experiments between research pilot plant (RPP) and industrial pilot plant (IPP) it was found that the general trends are the same and that the decisive process parameters identified in the RPP experiments could be successfully transferred to the IPP. Precise understanding of flow-conditions at the beginning of the heating zone are crucial to obtain a powder with the desired properties. By preheating and adjusting the geometry, recirculation leading to a broadened PSD could be effectively reduced and are supported by fluid dynamics simulations.

Hartmut Wiggers: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Moritz Loewenich: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hans Orthner: Investigation, Resources. Irenaeus Wlokas: Software, Supervision. Patrick Wollny: Software, Visualization. Julia Lyubina: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Stefan Bade: Investigation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Julia Lyubina reports financial support was provided by Evonik Operations GmbH. Stefan Bade reports financial support was provided by Evonik Operations GmbH. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: In memory of Vitalij K. Pecharsky, an outstanding scientist, wise and witty colleague.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the German Ministry of Economy and Climate Action [grant numbers 03EI3027A and 03EI3027B] The Authors thank the Interdisciplinary Center for Analytics on the Nanoscale (ICAN), UDE for TEM Microscopy. XRD measurements were kindly performed by Ali Sheikh.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.174061.
